threats.title: Relayer Fee Theft via Gas Griefing
threats.hypothesis_description: Relayers can steal message fees without executing the target contract by providing insufficient gas to `processMessage`.
threats.investigation_rationale: The EVM `call` opcode forwards 63/64 of available gas. By tuning the transaction gas limit, a relayer can ensure `gasleft()` is slightly below `_message.gasLimit`. The external call fails with Out-Of-Gas, the message is marked `RETRIABLE`, and the relayer pockets the fee.

proof_of_signal.pattern: gasLimit passed to _invokeMessageCall without verifying gasleft() >= _message.gasLimit

preconditions: Message target requires significant gas to execute

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Relayer controls transaction gas limit, forcing OOG in subcall while parent call succeeds
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Relayer earns _message.fee while spending minimal gas, forcing user to pay for retry

economic_questions: What is the minimum gas required to trigger the OOG in the subcall while keeping the parent call alive?
How much profit can be extracted per message?
validation_requirements: Test transaction with precise gas limit to trigger 63/64 rule OOG
Verify fee is paid to relayer when status becomes RETRIABLE

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: High ROI depending on fee size

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: When high-fee messages are in the mempool
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Degrades bridge reliability and steals user funds

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Loss of message fee + cost of retry

threats.title: Stale Canonical Mapping Persists After Token Migration
threats.hypothesis_description: In `changeBridgedToken`, the code attempts to delete the old token's mapping using `delete bridgedToCanonical[_btokenNew]`, which is a no-op. The old mapping `bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_]` persists.
threats.investigation_rationale: While `btokenOld_` is blacklisted for `sendToken`, the stale mapping could cause accounting errors or be exploited if other protocol features or upgrades rely on this mapping to determine canonical token addresses.
preconditions: Admin initiates a bridged token migration

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Stale state persists in bridgedToCanonical mapping
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Potential double-counting or integration failures in off-chain indexers or future upgrades

economic_questions: Does any other contract or off-chain system rely on bridgedToCanonical without checking the blacklist?
validation_requirements: Verify state of bridgedToCanonical[btokenOld_] after migration

threats.title: USDC Adapter Integration Failure due to Approval Mismatch
threats.hypothesis_description: The `USDCAdapter` uses `transferFrom` to pull tokens directly from the user during a bridge transfer. If a user only approves the `ERC20Vault`, the transaction will revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: Standard bridged tokens use `_burn` which bypasses allowances. The USDC adapter requires direct user approval. Frontends assuming standard vault approval is sufficient will cause user transactions to fail, wasting gas.
deployment_assumptions: Protocol uses USDCAdapter for bridging USDC
threats.config_evidence: DeployUSDCAdapter.s.sol configures USDCAdapter as the bridged token for USDC

proof_of_signal.pattern: usdc.transferFrom(_from, address(this), _amount) where _from is the user

preconditions: User approves ERC20Vault but not USDCAdapter

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: UX trap leading to failed transactions
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No direct profit, but causes gas loss for users and integration failures

economic_questions: How much gas is wasted per failed transaction?
validation_requirements: Test USDC bridging flow with only Vault approval

threats.title: Permanent Message Lock via Reverting Refund Address
threats.hypothesis_description: If a message's `refundTo` address is a smart contract that reverts on receiving ETH, `processMessage` will permanently revert, locking the message in `NEW` state.
threats.investigation_rationale: Because the message is stuck in `NEW` on the destination chain, it can never transition to `FAILED`. Consequently, it can never be recalled on the source chain, permanently locking the bridged assets (ETH and tokens).
preconditions: Message refundTo address is a contract that rejects ETH
Message has non-zero fee or value

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Push-over-pull pattern causes entire transaction to revert
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Self-griefing or trap for smart contract wallets; permanently locks assets

economic_questions: Can an attacker force a victim to use a reverting refundTo address?
validation_requirements: Test processMessage with a reverting refundTo contract
Verify message cannot be recalled on source chain

threats.title: Permanent Asset Lock via Reverting Source Owner on Recall
threats.hypothesis_description: If `_message.srcOwner` is a contract that cannot receive ETH, `recallMessage` will permanently revert, locking the user's tokens in the source vault.
threats.investigation_rationale: The `recallMessage` function uses a push pattern to return ETH to the `srcOwner`. If the transfer fails, the entire recall transaction reverts, preventing the user from recovering their bridged assets after a failed cross-chain execution.
preconditions: Message failed on destination chain (status FAILED)
srcOwner is a contract that rejects ETH

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Push-over-pull pattern causes recall to revert
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Self-griefing or trap for smart contract wallets; permanently locks assets

economic_questions: Are there common smart contract wallets that reject ETH without specific calldata?
validation_requirements: Test recallMessage with a reverting srcOwner contract

threats.title: Cryptographic Proof Bypass via Watchdog Unsuspend
threats.hypothesis_description: The `suspendMessages` function allows the `bridge_watchdog` to set `proofReceipt.receivedAt` to `block.timestamp` when unsuspending, which completely bypasses the Merkle proof verification in `processMessage`.
threats.investigation_rationale: A compromised watchdog can suspend and then unsuspend a completely fabricated message hash. Because `receivedAt != 0`, `processMessage` considers the message proven and executes it, allowing the attacker to mint arbitrary bridged tokens or drain bridge ETH.

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Watchdog can artificially set receivedAt, bypassing cryptographic cross-chain security
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Total drain of all bridge ETH and canonical vault tokens

economic_questions: Can this be executed atomically to prevent intervention?
validation_requirements: Test processing a fake message after suspend+unsuspend

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: Gas for suspend, unsuspend, and process

threats.title: Irrecoverable Token Loss via Destination Address Banning
threats.hypothesis_description: If a vault address is banned on the destination chain via `banAddress`, any in-flight messages to that vault are immediately marked `DONE` by `processMessage` without invoking the vault.
threats.investigation_rationale: Because the message status is set to `DONE`, it can never be recalled on the source chain (which requires `FAILED` status). The bridged tokens are permanently burned/locked on the source chain and never minted/released on the destination chain.

proof_of_signal.pattern: addressBanned[_message.to] marks message DONE without invocation

preconditions: Users have in-flight messages targeting that vault

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Admin action causes legitimate messages to complete without executing token logic
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: No profit, but causes severe user fund loss

economic_questions: Is there any recovery mechanism for messages marked DONE?
validation_requirements: Test processing a vault message while the vault is banned

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Loss of user trust and funds

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Total loss of bridged assets in that message
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Value of in-flight messages to banned address
